figures.gloss: The sovereign, unapproachable Creator of the universe who established an exclusive, unbreakable covenant with the seed of Jacob. He is not a universalist deity but the fiercely partisan God of the Israelites, governing history solely to test, punish, and ultimately redeem His chosen people.
figures.facet: He violently rejects theological universalism, such as the concept that 'God loves everyone.' His divine love is strictly ethnocentric, viewing the nations outside the Israelite bloodline as 'a drop in the bucket' or as active enemies of His cosmic order.
figures.provenance: Isaiah 40:15; 2 Esdras 6:54-56; foundational BHI street apologetics.

figures.gloss: The messiah of Israel, prophesied to return as a conquering king. Depicted explicitly as a Black man with 'hair like wool and feet like burnt brass,' he died exclusively for the salvation and restoration of the twelve tribes of Israel scattered in the diaspora.
figures.facet: He is stripped of his passive, turning-the-other-cheek 'white Jesus' iconography (often identified as Cesare Borgia). He is a militaristic, uncompromising liberator who will return to violently dismantle global white supremacy and establish an Israelite theocracy.
figures.provenance: Revelation 1:14-15; Daniel 10:5-6; 1 West camp doctrines (ISUPK, ICGJC).

figures.gloss: The twin brother of Jacob and the progenitor of the Edomites, mapped prophetically to modern-day people of European descent. Edom represents the global architects of systemic oppression, chattel slavery, and false religion.
figures.facet: Systemic racism and colonial history are not merely sociological phenomena but the literal, prophetic playing out of an ancient blood-feud between two ancestral wombs, making racial conflict deeply cosmic and inescapable until divine intervention.

figures.gloss: The central diagnostic text of the faith. It prophesies that if Israel broke God's law, they would suffer uniquely horrific curses—most notably, being shipped into bondage via slave ships, a fate mapped directly onto the Transatlantic Slave Trade.
figures.facet: It turns historical trauma into divine proof. The horrors of slavery and modern systemic marginalization are weaponized as empirical, forensic evidence of divine chosenness rather than mere victimhood.
figures.provenance: Deuteronomy 28:68; ubiquitous in BHI literature and street-corner exegesis.

figures.gloss: A prophetic and genealogical schema, formulated by Abba Bivens and the 1 West camp in the late 1960s, that identifies African Americans, West Indians, Native Americans, and Hispanics as the literal, genetic descendants of the Twelve Tribes of Israel. It is the standard visual doctrine of the 1West/ISUPK street-camp lineage specifically — the Commandment Keepers' rabbinic congregations and the Dimona-based African Hebrew Israelite Nation do not use this chart or its tribal assignments.
figures.facet: It actively re-engineers modern racial and ethnic categories imposed by European colonizers, replacing sociological labels like 'Black,' 'Hispanic,' or 'Native' with a unified, divinely ordained bloodline of cosmic royalty.
figures.provenance: Formulated by figures like Abba Bivens and the 1 West camp in the late 1960s; visually foundational to BHI practice.

figures.gloss: The profound, transformative realization of one's true identity as a biblical Israelite. It involves shedding 'slave names,' secular history, and mental colonization in favor of Torah observance, fringes, and covenantal awareness.
figures.facet: It reframes salvation as an epistemological break. One is not 'saved' merely by a confession of faith, but by literally remembering who they are, waking from the culturally induced amnesia of the diaspora.
figures.provenance: Zechariah 8:8; Romans 13:11; emphasis on 'knowing who you are' in BHI pedagogy.

figures.gloss: The impending, literal millennial reign on Earth where Yahawashi will rule from Jerusalem. In this era, the current global power structure will be inverted: the Israelites will rule as sovereigns, and the nations will go into servitude.
figures.facet: Heaven is not a non-corporeal realm of clouds and harps, but a brutal, physical geopolitical reversal on Earth where the historically enslaved become the masters, enacting divine vengeance and perfect law.

figures.gloss: Born into slavery in Maryland (1847), escaped in 1863 to serve as a Buffalo Soldier, then a railroad cook. In 1893 he began reporting visions that Black Americans descend from the lost tribes of Israel, and in 1896 founded the Church of God and Saints of Christ in Kansas — the earliest institutionally documented Black Hebrew Israelite body, with its own tabernacles, feast-keeping, and a 'Canaan Land' settlement bought in Suffolk, Virginia (1903).
figures.facet: The movement's first paper trail: unlike the doctrine's mythic time-depth, Crowdy's church has a founder with a birth certificate, a discharge paper, and an arrest record (twenty-two arrests) — history, not only prophecy, standing behind the identity-claim.
figures.provenance: Church of God and Saints of Christ institutional history; Elly M. Wynia, The Church of God and Saints of Christ: The Rise of Black Jews (1994).

figures.gloss: Self-taught in Hebrew and Yiddish, a former sailor who reported his own prophetic call while abroad, Cherry founded the Church of the Living God, the Pillar Ground of Truth for All Nations in Chattanooga, Tennessee, in 1886 — teaching that God, Jesus, Adam, and Eve were Black, and that African Americans are the true, dispossessed Israelites. A Garvey supporter, he fused early Black nationalism with Torah-Sabbath observance.
figures.facet: The movement's other origin point, working independently of Crowdy: two unconnected 1880s–90s visionaries arriving at the same identity-claim from different cities is itself the datum the roster had been missing.
figures.provenance: Howard Brotz, The Black Jews of Harlem (1964); standard histories of the Black Hebrew Israelite movement (Chattanooga, 1886 founding).

figures.gloss: A St. Kitts-born Harlem street-preacher (b. 1892) who in 1919 founded the Commandment Keepers Congregation, teaching that Black converts to Judaism were not converting but 'returning.' Unlike the street-camp wings, his congregation adopted Hebrew liturgy, Talmudic study, and rabbinic ordination — he founded the Israelite Rabbinical Academy in Brooklyn and trained Black rabbis.
figures.facet: The pole of the movement that resolves toward normative Judaism rather than away from it: where other camps sharpen a separate Israelite identity against white Christianity, Matthew's line assimilates into rabbinic practice itself, seeding today's Black Jewish synagogue lineages.
figures.provenance: Jacob S. Dorman, Chosen People: The Rise of American Black Israelite Religions (2013); Commandment Keepers congregational records.

figures.gloss: Chicago-born (1939) former steelworker who reported a 1966 revelation from the angel Gabriel commanding him to lead his people to the Promised Land. In 1967 some 350 followers settled first in Liberia under harsh conditions; from 1969 the community relocated by stages to Dimona, Israel, where the African Hebrew Israelite Nation still lives, several thousand strong at his death in 2014.
figures.facet: The one wing of the movement that converted eschatology into logistics: not a chart or a sermon but an actual multi-year transcontinental migration, making the promised return a documented itinerary rather than a doctrine awaiting fulfillment.
figures.provenance: John L. Jackson Jr., Thin Description: Ethnography and the African Hebrew Israelites of Jerusalem (2013); community and Israeli press accounts of the 1967–1970 migration.

figures.gloss: Organized public preaching by 1West/ISUPK-lineage camps: amplified scripture-reading from a stepladder or portable PA, call-and-response, and direct, often confrontational 'awakening' of passersby — the movement's most visible face in American cities since the late 1960s.
figures.facet: Doctrine as street theater rather than sanctuary practice: the camp does not wait for seekers to arrive at a temple, it takes the identity-claim and the Deuteronomy 28 diagnosis directly onto the sidewalk.
figures.provenance: Journalistic and ethnographic accounts of 1West/ISUPK street preaching (e.g., Southern Poverty Law Center reporting, Jackson's Thin Description); specific to that lineage, not the Commandment Keepers or Dimona wings.

figures.gloss: Observance of the seventh-day Sabbath and the biblical feasts (Passover, Tabernacles, and others per lineage) as covenant obligations incumbent on the restored Israelite — practiced, with variation, across the Crowdy, Cherry, Matthew, and Ben Ammi lineages alike.
figures.facet: The one practice nearly every wing of the movement shares despite doctrinal divergence: torah-calendar keeping functions as the lived, weekly proof-in-conduct of the identity the movement's theology asserts in the abstract.
figures.provenance: Church of God and Saints of Christ, Commandment Keepers, and African Hebrew Israelite congregational practice; Exodus 20:8-11, Leviticus 23.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The Most High and His explicitly commissioned, militaristic son, bound together to execute judgment on the earth.
Intra-tradition bond: The divine avenger of Israel stands in direct, cosmic opposition to the progenitor of systemic oppression.
Intra-tradition bond: The scriptural diagnostic of the curses provides the exact proof required to populate the genealogical chart.
Intra-tradition bond: The act of spiritual awakening is synonymous with finding one's specific place on the tribal chart.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both identify as the God of Abraham, but BHI theology explicitly confines his covenant to a specific genetic lineage, rejecting Christian universalism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Syncretic identification, but entirely recontextualized as a militaristic, Black liberator rather than the passive, universal savior of Western orthodoxy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are eschatological saviors who arrive specifically to violently purge the world of falsehood and restore a righteous empire for the chosen.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Edom functions as the systemic, geopolitical embodiment of deceit and evil, mirroring the Zoroastrian cosmic architect of chaos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a wicked, illegitimate power structure that falsely claims sovereignty over the earth and keeps the true divine seed in material bondage.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both use a mathematically precise ancestral matrix to map out the entirety of a people's spiritual and social reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both frame salvation not as acquiring something new, but as waking up to an innate, divine royal nature suppressed by an oppressive material matrix.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Buddhist awakening relies on realizing the self does not fundamentally exist; BHI awakening relies on realizing the self is an eternal, genealogical absolute.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an absolute cosmic law where deviation brings inevitable material and societal ruin, functioning as a historical diagnostic tool.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are warrior saviors tasked with returning to the earth to violently slaughter the unnatural monsters (or systemic oppressors) that prey on the people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a profound, physical renovation of the earth where the wicked are finally subjugated and the righteous inherit a tangible, purified world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions utilize strict genealogical mapping as the ultimate claim to social, spiritual, and territorial inheritance.

Era-kin: two contemporaneous early-20th-century systems re-assigning Black Americans a pre-slavery sacred genealogy — Moabite Moors in one, the tribes of Israel in the other — each replacing imposed racial labels with a covenant nationality.

Intra-tradition bond: standard histories name these two as the movement's independent twin founders — Cherry in Chattanooga (1886), Crowdy in Kansas (1896) — arriving at the same Israelite-identity claim without contact between them.
Intra-tradition bond: Ben Ammi's 1967–1970 migration through Liberia to Dimona is the millennial return doctrine enacted in real geography and years, not merely awaited.
Intra-tradition bond: the chart is the street ministry's core visual prop, unfurled and preached from at the camps that make up the movement's public face.
Intra-tradition bond: keeping the Sabbath and feasts is, in the movement's own terms, the lived proof of the identity recovered in the Awakening — practice as the completion of realization.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two early-to-mid-20th-century Black American prophet-founders who each built an enduring institution on a revealed alternate sacred history of Black identity, wholly independent doctrinal systems reaching structurally similar authority claims.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cherry was a documented Garvey supporter, folding UNIA-era Black nationalism directly into his 1886 congregation's theology — an attested historical link, not a structural analogy.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Matthew's Harlem preaching drew explicitly on the Garveyite pan-Africanism and Black nationalism circulating in the same 1920s streets Garvey's UNIA organized — an attested shared milieu, not mere resemblance.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the Commandment Keepers adopted normative rabbinic practice — Hebrew liturgy, Talmudic study, ordained rabbis — embracing Torah-observant Judaism far more literally than the movement's street-camp wings.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both diasporic movements read an ancestral homeland as a literal Zion to which the chosen must physically return — Ben Ammi's community enacted it via Liberia to Dimona, Rastafari holds it as aspirational repatriation to Ethiopia/Africa.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both root Sabbath-keeping directly in the same Exodus 20:8-11 commandment as the litmus test of covenant obedience — a shared textual source claimed by two entirely independent 19th–20th-century American movements.

dossier.essence: A family of African-American religious movements, emerging from the late 19th century (Frank Cherry's congregation, conventionally dated 1886; William S. Crowdy's Church of God and Saints of Christ, 1896), holding that Black Americans — and in some branches other peoples of the Americas — are literal genealogical descendants of the biblical Israelites. The spectrum runs from Torah-observant congregations influenced by rabbinic Judaism (Wentworth A. Matthew's Commandment Keepers, founded in Harlem in 1919) to the militant '1 West' street camps descending from Abba Bivens' 1960s Israeli School; there is no unified organization or creed.
dossier.cosmos: The framework is biblical monotheism read as racialized covenant history: the Transatlantic slave trade is identified as fulfillment of the curses of Deuteronomy 28 — especially the slave ships of verse 68 — so that collective suffering itself proves Israelite identity. In the militant camps, modern peoples are mapped onto biblical lineages, Europeans identified with Edom as the adversary, and history moves toward a literal earthly Kingdom in which the returned messiah restores Israelite sovereignty and inverts the present racial order. Afterlife doctrine is comparatively thin; the weight falls on corporate destiny within history.
dossier.practice: Practice varies by camp: Sabbath-keeping, biblical feasts and dietary law, fringed garments, adoption of Hebrew names, and the pedagogy of 'waking up' — learning one's tribal identity from charts correlating modern ethnic groups with the Twelve Tribes. The street wing is defined by confrontational public exegesis — loud, chart-based sidewalk preaching and debate — while congregational wings maintain synagogue-style worship. Authority is local and charismatic: teachers, schools, and camps rather than a clerical hierarchy.
dossier.canon: The Bible — typically the King James Version with the Apocrypha (2 Esdras matters to several camps) — read through the movement's identity hermeneutic; no additional unified scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds chosenness as theodicy — collective suffering converted into forensic proof of election, with identity-recovery itself functioning as salvation.
sources: Jacob S. Dorman, 'Chosen People: The Rise of American Black Israelite Religions' (2013)
James E. Landing, 'Black Judaism: Story of an American Movement' (2002)
John L. Jackson Jr., 'Thin Description: Ethnography and the African Hebrew Israelites of Jerusalem' (2013)
